title: Burning ships, smoke, and the limits of decarbonisation
summary: Conflict-related ship fires release vast quantities of uncombusted heavy fuel oil (HFO) into the atmosphere and marine environment. While owners obsess over marginal gains from LED lights and hull coatings to meet CII targets, a single attack can negate years of emissions reductions. The industry's focus on decimals ignores the larger environmental impact of geopolitical instability on maritime emissions.
content:
When emissions come from conflict, who pays? Sunil Kapoor investigates.

It was a Friday evening. I was still in the office, working late to meet the EU ETS compliance deadline for reporting emissions for the year 2025—ironically for a vessel that had been handed over long ago.

There had been endless discussions. Where was the missing one metric tonne of fuel? Why was there a difference of 0.5 tonnes of CO₂? And how did we end up with a 0.1 tCO₂ variation in the total EUAs calculated?

Emails, calls, and clarifications kept going back and forth between the ship, the verifier, and class. Each number was checked and rechecked.

To make matters more complicated, everything had to be uploaded in THETIS and then aligned again with MOHA.

What struck me most was not the numbers, but the process.

It is a system designed by very capable people, but for those who must implement it, it often feels unnecessarily complicated—and for me personally, it is a nightmare.

From a shipowner’s perspective, the focus today is simple.

After buying a vessel, most of our discussions are about one thing — how to reduce fuel consumption.

LED lights, better hull coatings, main engine optimisation, energy-saving devices — we keep looking for improvements.

And what do we finally achieve? A few tonnes of fuel saved. A small improvement in emissions. Just enough, perhaps, to maintain a good CII rating. And even that is not fully in our control.

A poorly planned voyage, waiting time at anchorage, or cargo issues can easily push the rating down again.

And yet, while we are debating decimals, ships are burning. Not metaphorically — literally.

Recent attacks in areas like the Red Sea and the Strait of Hormuz have shown how vulnerable merchant ships are. A burning ship with thick black smoke and high flames is not just a safety issue, it is also a major environmental event.

A typical handysize vessel may carry around 1,500 tonnes of fuel. Larger vessels even more. When such a ship burns uncontrolled, the emissions released in hours can undo months of careful effort.

As of today, more than 25 ships have been hit in this recent conflict — and close to 100 if we combine the Red Sea and Russia–Ukraine situation.

In reality, ships are easy targets. A burning ship is a powerful optic—it sends a different kind of message. And once fire starts, it spreads very fast. I have seen in my own experience how quickly a small spark can turn into a serious situation.

And this is not limited to ships.

Recent images from Tehran showed dark clouds and black rain after fuel depots were bombed. Millions of litres of fuel were burned. It is estimated that between 2.5m to 5.9m barrels of oil were lost, releasing close to 1.9m tonnes of CO₂.

In just a short period of conflict, emissions ran into millions of tonnes.

The war in Ukraine shows an even bigger impact.Estimates suggest around 230m tonnes of CO₂ equivalent emissions so far — more than several European countries combined.

These emissions come from burning infrastructure, fuel depots, pipelines, and the rebuilding that follows.

Global military activity itself is estimated to contribute around 5.5% of total emissions.

And yet, most of this is not properly reported or included in climate frameworks. Why this is the case is difficult to understand. It exists — but outside the system.

In shipping, we measure everything. We reconcile 0.1 tonnes. We question small differences.

But emissions from conflict — running into millions of tonnes — are not treated the same way. This is where the imbalance is clear.

The industry is now moving towards net zero under the guidance of the International Maritime Organization, with further discussions expected later this year.

The direction is clear. The intent is right. But there is also a cost — and this is the real impact.

Today, it is estimated that under EU ETS, carbon cost is roughly €60–100 per tonne of CO₂. This adds around $2–10 per tonne of cargo in bulk shipping.

Going forward, with green fuels costing two to four times conventional fuel, freight costs could increase by $10–40 per tonne.

For consumers, this may look small. A few dollars more on a product. 1–3% increase in retail goods. $5–15 more per tonne for food grains. $100–300 increase in heavy goods like cars. But across global trade, these small increases add up. They create inflationary pressure. They increase the cost of energy and raw materials, and they impact developing economies the most.

The cost of decarbonisation does not stay with the shipowner.

It is passed on. In simple terms, freight increases and the cost of transporting things in general goes up. And finally, the consumer pays.

But my simple question is: when emissions come from conflict — who pays then? No one. They are not priced, not reported, and not accounted for. They simply go into the atmosphere.

This is not an argument against decarbonisation. It is necessary and will continue. But it cannot be looked at in isolation. Because in the end, the atmosphere does not differentiate between emissions from compliance and emissions from conflict, but our systems clearly do.

We measure grams with precision. We ignore tonnes without question. And that is the real gap.
